It is not called business. This is investing. What you consider when it comes to investing is the relationship between risk and return. A well-diversified investment portfolio reduces your exposure to risk. Money held in Piggy Flex (not Safe lock, where you are paid upfront) can be accessible at any time. While you don't have a need for the money, it earns interest until you need it. Also, Safelock is not a good option for me. Note, this is my POV, I want to have access to my cash when I need it. So, never Safelock any money you would in a short time. In business, cash is King, and the short turnover period is an indicator of profitability. Again, leaving your money in PiggyVest is not business. It is savings, which earns interest.

500,000 Naira minus 4 Naira is 499,996 Naira. Saving 500,000 Naira in Nigeria is tough due to low incomes and high living costs. With a minimum wage of 70,000 Naira monthly, it takes over 7 months to save that amount, and inflation at 23% in May 2025 erodes value. Budgeting, using apps like PiggyVest, or joining savings groups like Ajo can help. Side hustles also boost income. Still, economic challenges make it hard for many.

We're now pretty far off the original discussion of income, but here's some data: Mobile: * Nigeria's usage gap (# connected but not using mobile internet) has been stagnant for the last 5 years. About 60% of those with coverage do not use mobile internet. (GSMA) * Overall smartphone affordability in rural areas has fallen off a cliff. Handsets now costs 99% of monthly income for poorest 20% vs. 55% a couple years ago (GMSA) * Smartphones sales overall in Nigeria have been lagging rest of Africa (1% vs 3% YoY). Higher end brands like Samsung fell 30%. (Tecno) Food Insecurity: * There's been almost a doubling of those who are food insecure in Nigeria since 2023 (UN) * 40%+ food inflation meaning much less of wallet can go towards other things (NBS) Exports: * Both oil and natural gas exports have fallen on a volumes basis over time. * Oil is at a lower barrels per day than its historical average and about 60% of its production 10 years ago. * Natural gas exports has been steadily dropping since 2021 in terms of cubic meters. Savings / Income ('23 and '24 Piggyvest Surveys): * Those saving every month dropped 17% YoY. Those with an emergency funds dropped 4% * Earners above 500k naira dropped 8% YoY. No income responses increased 8%.

Safety-Related Pros and Cons of Laminated Side Windows Recently, many new vehicles have been adopting laminated glass for side windows. Laminated glass consists of two layers of glass with a plastic layer (PVB) sandwiched between them. While it has long been standard for windshields, its use in side windows is increasing to enhance crash safety. Below are the safety-related advantages and disadvantages. (Note: This information is based on general automotive industry standards and research, and may vary by specific vehicle model.) Advantages Crash Protection and Debris Prevention: Laminated glass doesn’t shatter into pieces upon impact, maintaining its shape and reducing the risk of passengers being injured by glass shards or ejected from the vehicle. This is particularly effective in rollover accidents, helping to keep body parts inside and increasing survival rates. UV Protection and Added Safety: The plastic layer blocks up to 99% of UV rays, reducing skin damage and interior heat buildup, which indirectly contributes to safety by reducing driver fatigue. Noise Reduction for Improved Focus: It reduces wind and road noise, enhancing driver concentration and indirectly helping prevent accidents. Enhanced Security: Laminated glass is harder to break, making it more resistant to theft or break-in attempts, improving the safety of parked vehicles. Disadvantages Difficulty in Emergency Escape: Laminated glass is highly durable and resistant to breaking, even with standard escape tools (e.g., window-breaking hammers). In emergencies like submersion or fire, this can make escape nearly impossible, increasing survival risks. This has become a significant concern with recent vehicles. Increased Weight Affecting Handling: Laminated glass is about 25% heavier than tempered glass, increasing the vehicle’s overall weight. This can reduce fuel efficiency and slightly affect stability during high-speed driving or sudden braking due to changes in the center of gravity. Overall, laminated side windows significantly improve crash safety but pose challenges for emergency escapes. Manufacturers are working on solutions like specialized escape tools. When purchasing a vehicle, it’s recommended to check the specific model’s specifications and consult with experts if needed.

The #Pokémon Trading Card Game Pocket app has replaced the #HoOh and Lugia EX card illustrations from its new "Wisdom of Sea and Sky" set after an internal review confirmed that incorrect reference materials had been provided to the illustrator, leading to plagiarism accusations from artist #lanjiujiu. The original immersive art, which resembled lanjiujiu's 2021 fan art, was temporarily removed on July 30 and replaced with a "New Art Coming Soon" message before the updated designs were added. The #Johto-themed set was released the same day. Despite some controversy, the mobile app, created by The #Pokemon Company, Creatures Inc., and DeNA, has generated over $100 million since its launch in October 2024.

1/5 — Early Experiments and Skepticism Early attempts to make Pokémon cards into NFTs were unofficial and raised IP concerns. In February 2024, the Pokémon Company clarified that it “has not developed or approved” any NFTs related to its brand and warned that any NFTs using Pokémon intellectual property are “unofficial and in no way affiliated”. Fans had speculated that the new Pokémon Trading Card Game Pocket app might integrate NFTs, but the developer insisted it would focus on digital card collecting without blockchain. This early phase was marked by rumours, unauthorised projects, and skepticism from traditional Pokémon fans.

If you are a freelancer or entrepreneur, this particular hack has helped me save money so easily. 1. Save anything between 10-20% from any gigs you are paid for immediately the money lands your account and before you start to spend it. 2. Save 5-7k on a daily basis. With platforms like Piggyvest this can be so seamless. 3. The 10-20% from your gigs, lock it and let it grow so you don’t tempt yourself to think you have any money (you have to practically pretend that money doesn’t currently exist). For me, I lock it up for a year to get maximum interest (for instance, 20% interest if I lock money up for 1 year, meaning 200k becomes 240k) 4. Rinse and repeat this and make it a hobby. 5. Check how much you have saved in 3 months. Many of you who think you aren’t even millionaires will start to believe.

Start Small With Investing One big mistake people make is waiting until they have plenty of money before they start investing. Truth is, investing is less about the amount and more about the habit. Even ₦10k / $10 a month into mutual funds, savings bonds, or an ETF builds consistency. That habit trains your mind to think long-term and creates discipline around money. 👉🏽 The earlier you start, the more time your money has to grow. Compound interest is slow at first, but years down the line, it works like magic. Don’t fall for the lie that only the rich invest. The rich became rich because they started where they were. Pick a safe beginner option — e.g. Cowrywise, PiggyVest (Nigeria) or Vanguard ETFs (US/UK). Automate your monthly contribution, even if it’s small. May your little investments grow into a financial harvest you never imagined. If you started investing just ₦5k–₦10k monthly 3 years ago, how much do you think you’d have by now? 👀 #finacialtips

Here's a beginner's path to build toward deploying capital like in the post, leveraging your grit and learning speed. Start small, scale up. 1. **Educate yourself**: Take free online courses on Coursera/YouTube (e.g., entrepreneurship, finance basics). Read books like "Rich Dad Poor Dad" or Nigerian blogs on business. Focus on simple models like freelancing or reselling. 2. **Gain experience via side hustles**: Freelance on Upwork/Fiverr (writing, marketing) or local gigs (tutoring, delivery). Aim to earn ₦50k-100k/month initially. Use apps like PiggyVest/Cowrywise to save 20-30% automatically. 3. **Source capital**: Save from hustles; ask family/friends for small loans (₦100k start). Apply for grants (e.g., Tony Elumelu Foundation) or crowdfunding on GoFundMe. Avoid high-interest loans early. 4. **Test a model**: Start with low-capital ideas like dropshipping or service biz (e.g., social media management). Invest small (₦50k) in tested opportunities, track returns. 5. **Scale**: Reinvest profits, network on LinkedIn/X for mentors. Aim for 10-20% monthly returns initially through compounding. Stay consistent; it takes 6-12 months to build momentum. Research risks thoroughly.

I realized today that most of us want to make money, but most people won’t really get it because they have no reference point. So they don’t really understand fully what it is they want. Of course I’d explain - Wealth means different things to different people; and these meaning is usually influenced by our past & present experiences - so what we want is somewhat in between. If your parents never built a house, and you build a house - they would say you have achieved a whole lot Yes you have (building a house is not easy in this economy): but based off their reference point they’d wonder why you still work hard and what else you want out of life. This is the point where they say - “relax, you have achieved more than most people” This is caused by reference points; you on the other hand have seen richer people - so you tell them: “no mummy, I still need to push” You feel my drift? The higher your reference point the bigger your appetite, the bigger your appetite the big the opportunity you’d chase This is why you go on to make 10x what your parents made. Now here is where the problem is - your reference point too is small when compared to another person’s reference point. This morning I got a terrible hit to my reference point after I went through Temi Otedola’s tiktok: @temiotedola I watched her life, compared it to where I am now and felt completely far behind. There is a life so full of ease and luxury it is absolutely mind blowing. And I don’t have it now, and I want it. …and I saw how different my life is when compared with what she has. Then I imagined what her reference point would be and shuddered. Mr Eazi is a hard working guy, great guy - but he left music and became part owner of a billion dollar business after he started hanging with the Otedola kids. My guess is his reference point changed. Reference point controls the kind of business you do, the level of wealth you chase and ultimately the level of wealth you get. I just realized that a few hours ago, and it has sent cold shivers down my spine. I was recently watching a series called: “House of Usher” - It is a horror story about a billionaire. When any of his kids comes of age they would come to pitch a business to him and if he likes it he funds it. His last son comes to him and pitches a night club: his idea is grand but the man waves it off. Then said (I will paraphrase) - “an Usher builds something that changes the world, not a business that marks up Glenfiddich” - It is a movie but it just hit me; most of the times the reference point we have is so small all we do is buy and sell. Then others with a bigger point of reference build Paystack…Flutterwave…MoniePoint…Piggyvest…Andela. It is insane. A big real estate tycoon once advised me to move to Lekki Phase 1. He said that was what he did that made him rich. Waking up and seeing rich people… …and seeing them do rich people things; it somehow stops you from playing too small. Now I fully get it; it increases your point of reference. If you stay around people where the biggest achievement is a nice car and a nice house that would be all you would ever want. Point of reference is something else and boy! Teni Otedola’s tiktok is a whole lot of point of reference - in one video she did a walk through of her father’s house and it made it clear to me what I want when I fantasize about being rich. When you have not been near real wealth before, you know you want it but somehow you still don’t know what it is you want. Until you see it on a tiktok account like I did today and you know - clearly - what it is you want, and how frigging beautiful it is.

NTB stands for Nigerian Treasury Bills—short-term government securities issued by the Central Bank of Nigeria to raise funds, with maturities of 91, 182, or 364 days. They're low-risk and offer fixed returns. To find a financial advisor in Nigeria: - Contact banks like UBA or FirstBank for in-house services. - Use apps like PiggyVest or Cowrywise, which offer guidance. - Search platforms like Upwork for freelancers, or firms like FSDH Asset Management. - Look for certified pros via SEC Nigeria's website or local directories. Always verify credentials and consider fees.

To start investing in Nigerian Treasury Bills (NTBs): 1. Use apps like PiggyVest or Cowrywise: Download, sign up, verify ID, fund your wallet, and select NTB options. Minimum: ₦100–₦1,000. 2. Or via banks (e.g., UBA, FirstBank): Open an account, apply for NTBs. Minimum often ₦100,000+. Latest rates (Sep 3, 2025 auction): 91-day yield ~15.94%, 182-day ~16.81%, 364-day ~21.49%. Returns depend on tenor and amount; they're discounted upfront. Consult a financial advisor for risks.
